## 7. AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md Context

> **This is NOT the Next.js you know** -- this version has breaking changes -- APIs, conventions, and file structure may all differ from your training data. Read the relevant guide in `node_modules/next/dist/docs/` before writing any code. Heed deprecation notices.

## 8. Key Next.js 16.2 Docs Findings

The docs in `node_modules/next/dist/docs/` revealed several important things about this canary version:

### Server Actions
- Documented at `08-updating-data.mdx`. Server Actions are created with the `'use server'` directive, either at the top of a file or inline in a function body.
- They automatically receive `FormData` when used in `<form action={...}>`.
- Can use `useActionState` for pending states and validation errors.
- Can use `useFormStatus` for button-level pending states.

### Cache APIs (Next.js 16 specific):

These are the key APIS available from `next/cache`:

| API | Context | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **`updateTag(tag)`** | Server Actions only | Immediately expires cache -- next request waits for fresh data. Designed for read-your-own-writes (e.g., creating a post). |
| **`revalidateTag(tag, profile)`** | Server Actions & Route Handlers | With `profile="max"` uses stale-while-revalidate. Without profile is deprecated. |
| **`revalidatePath(path)`** | Server Actions & Route Handlers | Invalidates a specific page/layout path. |
| **`refresh()`** | Server Actions only | Refreshes the client router (like `router.refresh()`). |
| **`cacheTag('tag')`** | Inside `'use cache'` functions | Tags cached data for later revalidation. Requires `cacheComponents: true` in config. |

### Version 16 Breaking Changes (relevant ones):
- **`revalidateTag`** now requires a second argument (`profile="max"`) -- the single-argument form is deprecated.
- **`updateTag`** is a new function, Server Actions only, for read-your-own-writes scenarios.
- **`refresh`** is a new function imported from `next/cache`, Server Actions only.
- **`cacheComponents`** replaces `experimental.dynamicIO` -- to enable Cache Components (`'use cache'` directive, `cacheTag`, `cacheLife`), you need `cacheComponents: true` in `next.config.ts`.
- **`cacheTag` and `cacheLife`** are now stable (no longer need the `unstable_` prefix).
- `params` and `searchParams` in pages/layouts are now async only (must be awaited).
- `middleware` is deprecated in favor of `proxy`.

### Upgrade to version 16 guide
- The full version-16 upgrade guide is at `node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app/02-guides/upgrading/version-16.mdx`.

## Summary

This is a **fresh, minimal Next.js 16.2.0-canary.41** project with no existing implementation of posts, Server Actions, or caching. The page at `/` explicitly asks for a Server Action for creating posts with cache invalidation to be implemented. The relevant Next.js 16 docs show that `updateTag` is the recommended approach for Server Actions where you need read-your-own-writes semantics (create a post, immediately see it), and it should be imported from `next/cache`. If Component-level caching with `'use cache'` is desired, the `cacheComponents` flag must first be enabled in `next.config.ts`.

2	title: How to upgrade to version 16
3	nav_title: Version 16
4	description: Upgrade your Next.js Application from Version 15 to 16.
5	---
6	
7	{/* The content of this doc is shared between the app and pages router. You can use the `<PagesOnly>Content</PagesOnly>` component to add content that is specific to the Pages Router. Any shared content should not be wrapped in a component. */}
8	
9	## Upgrading from 15 to 16
10	
11	### Using AI Agents with Next.js DevTools MCP
12	
13	If you're using an AI coding assistant that supports the [Model Context Protocol (MCP)](https://modelcontextprotocol.io), you can use the **Next.js DevTools MCP** to automate the upgrade process and migration tasks.
14	
15	#### Setup
16	
17	Add the following configuration to your MCP client, for each coding agent you can read [this section](https://github.com/vercel/next-devtools-mcp#mcp-client-configuration) for configuration details.
18	
19	**example:**
20	
21	```json filename=".mcp.json"
22	{
23	  "mcpServers": {
24	    "next-devtools": {
25	      "command": "npx",
26	      "args": ["-y", "next-devtools-mcp@latest"]
27	    }
28	  }
29	}
30	```
31	
32	For more information, visit the [`next-devtools-mcp`](https://github.com/vercel/next-devtools-mcp) documentation to configure with your MCP client.
33	
34	> **Note:** Using `next-devtools-mcp@latest` ensures that your MCP client will always use the latest version of the Next.js DevTools MCP server.
35	
36	#### Example Prompts
37	
38	Once configured, you can use natural language prompts to upgrade your Next.js app:
39	
40	**To upgrade to Next.js 16:**
41	
42	Connect to your coding agent and then prompt:
43	
44	```txt
45	Next Devtools, help me upgrade my Next.js app to version 16
46	```
47	
48	**To migrate to Cache Components (after upgrading to v16):**
49	
50	Connect to your coding agent and then prompt:
51	
52	```txt
53	Next Devtools, migrate my Next.js app to cache components
54	```
55	
56	Learn more in the documentation [here](/docs/app/guides/mcp).
57	
58	### Using the Codemod
59	
60	To update to Next.js version 16, you can use the `upgrade` [codemod](/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#160):
61	
62	```bash package="pnpm"
63	pnpm dlx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest
64	```
65	
66	```bash package="npm"
67	npx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest
68	```
69	
70	```bash package="yarn"
71	yarn dlx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest
72	```
73	
74	```bash package="bun"
75	bunx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest
76	```
77	
78	The [codemod](/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#160) is able to:
79	
80	- Update `next.config.js` to use the new `turbopack` configuration
81	- Migrate from `next lint` to the ESLint CLI
82	- Migrate from deprecated `middleware` convention to `proxy`
83	- Remove `unstable_` prefix from stabilized APIs
84	- Remove `experimental_ppr` Route Segment Config from pages and layouts
85	
86	If you prefer to do it manually, install the latest Next.js and React versions:
87	
88	```bash package="pnpm"
89	pnpm add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest
90	```
91	
92	```bash package="npm"
93	npm install next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest
94	```
95	
96	```bash package="yarn"
97	yarn add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest
98	```
99	
100	```bash package="bun"
101	bun add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest
102	```
103	
104	If you are using TypeScript, ensure you also upgrade `@types/react` and `@types/react-dom` to their latest versions.
105	
106	## Node.js runtime and browser support
107	
108	| Requirement   | Change / Details                                                   |
109	| ------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------ |
110	| Node.js 20.9+ | Minimum version now `20.9.0` (LTS); Node.js 18 no longer supported |
111	| TypeScript 5+ | Minimum version now `5.1.0`                                        |
112	| Browsers      | Chrome 111+, Edge 111+, Firefox 111+, Safari 16.4+                 |
113	
114	## Turbopack by default
115	
116	Starting with **Next.js 16**, Turbopack is stable and used by default with `next dev` and `next build`
117	
118	Previously you had to enable Turbopack using `--turbopack`, or `--turbo`.
119	
120	```json filename="package.json"
121	{
122	  "scripts": {
123	    "dev": "next dev --turbopack",
124	    "build": "next build --turbopack",
125	    "start": "next start"
126	  }
127	}
128	```
129	
130	This is no longer necessary. You can update your `package.json` scripts:
131	
132	```json filename="package.json"
133	{
134	  "scripts": {
135	    "dev": "next dev",
136	    "build": "next build",
137	    "start": "next start"
138	  }
139	}
140	```
141	
142	If your project has a [custom `webpack`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/webpack) configuration and you run `next build` (which now uses Turbopack by default), the build will **fail** to prevent misconfiguration issues.
143	
144	You have a few different ways to address this:
145	
146	- **Use Turbopack anyway:** Run with `next build --turbopack` to build using Turbopack and ignore your `webpack` config.
147	- **Switch to Turbopack fully:** Migrate your `webpack` config to Turbopack-compatible options.
148	- **Keep using Webpack:** Use the `--webpack` flag to opt out of Turbopack and build with Webpack.
149	
150	> **Good to know**: If you see failing builds because a `webpack` configuration was found, but you don't define one yourself, it is likely that a plugin is adding a `webpack` option
151	
152	### Opting out of Turbopack
153	
154	If you need to continue using Webpack, you can opt out with the `--webpack` flag. For example, to use Turbopack in development but Webpack for production builds:
155	
156	```json filename="package.json"
157	{
158	  "scripts": {
159	    "dev": "next dev",
160	    "build": "next build --webpack",
161	    "start": "next start"
162	  }
163	}
164	```
165	
166	We recommend using Turbopack for development and production. Submit a comment to this [thread](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/77721), if you are unable to switch to Turbopack.
167	
168	### Turbopack configuration location
169	
170	The `experimental.turbopack` configuration is out of experimental.
171	
172	```ts filename="next.config.ts"
173	import type { NextConfig } from 'next'
174	
175	// Next.js 15 - experimental.turbopack
176	const nextConfig: NextConfig = {
177	  experimental: {
178	    turbopack: {
179	      // options
180	    },
181	  },
182	}
183	
184	export default nextConfig
185	```
186	
187	You can use it as a top-level `turbopack` option:
188	
189	```ts filename="next.config.ts"
190	import type { NextConfig } from 'next'
191	
192	// Next.js 16 - turbopack at the top level of nextConfig
193	const nextConfig: NextConfig = {
194	  turbopack: {
195	    // options
196	  },
197	}
198	
199	export default nextConfig
200	```

1	---
2	title: Caching in Next.js
3	nav_title: Caching
4	description: An overview of caching mechanisms in Next.js.
5	---
6	
7	Next.js improves your application's performance and reduces costs by caching rendering work and data requests. This page provides an in-depth look at Next.js caching mechanisms, the APIs you can use to configure them, and how they interact with each other.
8	
9	> **Good to know**: This page helps you understand how Next.js works under the hood but is **not** essential knowledge to be productive with Next.js. Most of Next.js' caching heuristics are determined by your API usage and have defaults for the best performance with zero or minimal configuration. If you instead want to jump to examples, [start here](/docs/app/getting-started/fetching-data).
10	
11	## Overview
12	
13	Here's a high-level overview of the different caching mechanisms and their purpose:
14	
15	| Mechanism                                   | What                       | Where  | Purpose                                         | Duration                        |
16	| ------------------------------------------- | -------------------------- | ------ | ----------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------- |
17	| [Request Memoization](#request-memoization) | Return values of functions | Server | Re-use data in a React Component tree           | Per-request lifecycle           |
18	| [Data Cache](#data-cache)                   | Data                       | Server | Store data across user requests and deployments | Persistent (can be revalidated) |
19	| [Full Route Cache](#full-route-cache)       | HTML and RSC payload       | Server | Reduce rendering cost and improve performance   | Persistent (can be revalidated) |
20	| [Router Cache](#client-side-router-cache)   | RSC Payload                | Client | Reduce server requests on navigation            | User session or time-based      |
21	
22	By default, Next.js will cache as much as possible to improve performance and reduce cost. This means routes are **statically rendered** and data requests are **cached** unless you opt out. The diagram below shows the default caching behavior: when a route is statically rendered at build time and when a static route is first visited.
23	
24	<Image
25	  alt="Diagram showing the default caching behavior in Next.js for the four mechanisms, with HIT, MISS and SET at build time and when a route is first visited."
26	  srcLight="/docs/light/caching-overview.png"
27	  srcDark="/docs/dark/caching-overview.png"
28	  width="1600"
29	  height="1179"
30	/>
31	
32	Caching behavior changes depending on whether the route is statically or dynamically rendered, data is cached or uncached, and whether a request is part of an initial visit or a subsequent navigation. Depending on your use case, you can configure the caching behavior for individual routes and data requests.
33	
34	Fetch caching is **not** supported in `proxy`. Any fetches done inside of your `proxy` will be uncached.
35	
36	## Rendering Strategies
37	
38	To understand how caching works in Next.js, it's helpful to understand the rendering strategies available. The rendering strategy determines when your route's HTML is generated, which directly impacts what can be cached.
39	
40	### Static Rendering
41	
42	With Static Rendering, routes are rendered at **build time** or in the background after [data revalidation](/docs/app/guides/incremental-static-regeneration). The result is cached and can be reused across requests. Static routes are fully cached in the [Full Route Cache](#full-route-cache).
43	
44	### Dynamic Rendering
45	
46	With Dynamic Rendering, routes are rendered at **request time**. This happens when your route uses request-specific information like cookies, headers, or search params.
47	
48	A route becomes dynamic when it uses any of these APIs:
49	
50	- [`cookies`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cookies)
51	- [`headers`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/headers)
52	- [`connection`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/connection)
53	- [`draftMode`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/draft-mode)
54	- [`searchParams` prop](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page#searchparams-optional)
55	- [`unstable_noStore`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/unstable_noStore)
56	- [`fetch`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/fetch) with `{ cache: 'no-store' }`
57	
58	Dynamic routes are not cached in the Full Route Cache, but can still use the [Data Cache](#data-cache) for data requests.
59	
60	> **Good to know**: You can use [Cache Components](/docs/app/getting-started/cache-components) to mix static and dynamic rendering within the same route.
61	
62	## Request Memoization
63	
64	Next.js extends the [`fetch` API](#fetch) to automatically **memoize** requests that have the same URL and options. This means you can call a fetch function for the same data in multiple places in a React component tree while only executing it once.
65	
66	<Image
67	  alt="Deduplicated Fetch Requests"
68	  srcLight="/docs/light/deduplicated-fetch-requests.png"
69	  srcDark="/docs/dark/deduplicated-fetch-requests.png"
70	  width="1600"
71	  height="857"
72	/>
73	
74	For example, if you need to use the same data across a route (e.g. in a Layout, Page, and multiple components), you do not have to fetch data at the top of the tree, and forward props between components. Instead, you can fetch data in the components that need it without worrying about the performance implications of making multiple requests across the network for the same data.
75	
76	```tsx filename="app/example.tsx" switcher
77	async function getItem() {
78	  // The `fetch` function is automatically memoized and the result
79	  // is cached
80	  const res = await fetch('https://.../item/1')
81	  return res.json()
82	}
83	
84	// This function is called twice, but only executed the first time
85	const item = await getItem() // cache MISS
86	
87	// The second call could be anywhere in your route
88	const item = await getItem() // cache HIT
89	```
90	
91	```jsx filename="app/example.js" switcher
92	async function getItem() {
93	  // The `fetch` function is automatically memoized and the result
94	  // is cached
95	  const res = await fetch('https://.../item/1')
96	  return res.json()
97	}
98	
99	// This function is called twice, but only executed the first time
100	const item = await getItem() // cache MISS
101	
102	// The second call could be anywhere in your route
103	const item = await getItem() // cache HIT
104	```
105	
106	**How Request Memoization Works**
107	
108	<Image
109	  alt="Diagram showing how fetch memoization works during React rendering."
110	  srcLight="/docs/light/request-memoization.png"
111	  srcDark="/docs/dark/request-memoization.png"
112	  width="1600"
113	  height="742"
114	/>
115	
116	- While rendering a route, the first time a particular request is called, its result will not be in memory and it'll be a cache `MISS`.
117	- Therefore, the function will be executed, and the data will be fetched from the external source, and the result will be stored in memory.
118	- Subsequent function calls of the request in the same render pass will be a cache `HIT`, and the data will be returned from memory without executing the function.
119	- Once the route has been rendered and the rendering pass is complete, memory is "reset" and all request memoization entries are cleared.
120	
121	> **Good to know**:
122	>
123	> - Request memoization is a React feature, not a Next.js feature. It's included here to show how it interacts with the other caching mechanisms.
124	> - Memoization only applies to the `GET` method in `fetch` requests.
125	> - Memoization only applies to the React Component tree, this means:
126	>   - It applies to `fetch` requests in `generateMetadata`, `generateStaticParams`, Layouts, Pages, and other Server Components.
127	>   - It doesn't apply to `fetch` requests in Route Handlers as they are not a part of the React component tree.
128	> - For cases where `fetch` is not suitable (e.g. some database clients, CMS clients, or GraphQL clients), you can use the [React `cache` function](#react-cache-function) to memoize functions.
129	
130	### Duration
131	
132	The cache lasts the lifetime of a server request until the React component tree has finished rendering.
133	
134	### Revalidating
135	
136	Since the memoization is not shared across server requests and only applies during rendering, there is no need to revalidate it.
137	
138	### Opting out
139	
140	Memoization only applies to the `GET` method in `fetch` requests, other methods, such as `POST` and `DELETE`, are not memoized. This default behavior is a React optimization and we do not recommend opting out of it.
141	
142	To manage individual requests, you can use the [`signal`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/AbortController/signal) property from [`AbortController`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/AbortController).
143	
144	```js filename="app/example.js"
145	const { signal } = new AbortController()
146	fetch(url, { signal })
147	```
148	
149	## Data Cache
150	
151	Next.js has a built-in Data Cache that **persists** the result of data fetches across incoming **server requests** and **deployments**. This is possible because Next.js extends the native `fetch` API to allow each request on the server to set its own persistent caching semantics.
152	
153	> **Good to know**: In the browser, the `cache` option of `fetch` indicates how a request will interact with the browser's HTTP cache, in Next.js, the `cache` option indicates how a server-side request will interact with the server's Data Cache.
154	
155	You can use the [`cache`](#fetch-optionscache) and [`next.revalidate`](#fetch-optionsnextrevalidate) options of `fetch` to configure the caching behavior.
156	
157	In development mode, `fetch` data is [reused for Hot Module Replacement (HMR)](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/fetch#fetch-default-auto-no-store-and-cache-no-store-not-showing-fresh-data-in-development), and caching options are ignored for [hard refreshes](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/fetch#hard-refresh-and-caching-in-development).
158	
159	**How the Data Cache Works**
160	
161	<Image
162	  alt="Diagram showing how cached and uncached fetch requests interact with the Data Cache. Cached requests are stored in the Data Cache, and memoized, uncached requests are fetched from the data source, not stored in the Data Cache, and memoized."
163	  srcLight="/docs/light/data-cache.png"
164	  srcDark="/docs/dark/data-cache.png"
165	  width="1600"
166	  height="661"
167	/>
168	
169	- The first time a `fetch` request with the `'force-cache'` option is called during rendering, Next.js checks the Data Cache for a cached response.
170	- If a cached response is found, it's returned immediately and [memoized](#request-memoization).
171	- If a cached response is not found, the request is made to the data source, the result is stored in the Data Cache, and memoized.
172	- For uncached data (e.g. no `cache` option defined or using `{ cache: 'no-store' }`), the result is always fetched from the data source, and memoized.
173	- Whether the data is cached or uncached, the requests are always memoized to avoid making duplicate requests for the same data during a React render pass.
174	
175	> **Differences between the Data Cache and Request Memoization**
176	>
177	> While both caching mechanisms help improve performance by re-using cached data, the Data Cache is persistent across incoming requests and deployments, whereas memoization only lasts the lifetime of a request.
178	
179	### Duration
180	
181	The Data Cache is persistent across incoming requests and deployments unless you revalidate or opt-out.
182	
183	### Revalidating
184	
185	Cached data can be revalidated in two ways, with:
186	
187	- **Time-based Revalidation**: Revalidate data after a certain amount of time has passed and a new request is made. This is useful for data that changes infrequently and freshness is not as critical.
188	- **On-demand Revalidation:** Revalidate data based on an event (e.g. form submission). On-demand revalidation can use a tag-based or path-based approach to revalidate groups of data at once. This is useful when you want to ensure the latest data is shown as soon as possible (e.g. when content from your headless CMS is updated).
189	
190	#### Time-based Revalidation
191	
192	To revalidate data at a timed interval, you can use the `next.revalidate` option of `fetch` to set the cache lifetime of a resource (in seconds).
193	
194	```js
195	// Revalidate at most every hour
196	fetch('https://...', { next: { revalidate: 3600 } })
197	```
198	
199	Alternatively, you can use [Route Segment Config options](#segment-config-options) to configure all `fetch` requests in a segment or for cases where you're not able to use `fetch`.
200	
201	**How Time-based Revalidation Works**
202	
203	<Image
204	  alt="Diagram showing how time-based revalidation works, after the revalidation period, stale data is returned for the first request, then data is revalidated."
205	  srcLight="/docs/light/time-based-revalidation.png"
206	  srcDark="/docs/dark/time-based-revalidation.png"
207	  width="1600"
208	  height="1252"
209	/>
210	
211	- The first time a fetch request with `revalidate` is called, the data will be fetched from the external data source and stored in the Data Cache.
212	- Any requests that are called within the specified timeframe (e.g. 60-seconds) will return the cached data.
213	- After the timeframe, the next request will still return the cached (now stale) data.
214	  - Next.js will trigger a revalidation of the data in the background.
215	  - Once the data is fetched successfully, Next.js will update the Data Cache with the fresh data.
216	  - If the background revalidation fails, the previous data will be kept unaltered.
217	
218	This is similar to [**stale-while-revalidate**](https://web.dev/articles/stale-while-revalidate) behavior.
219	
220	#### On-demand Revalidation
221	
222	Data can be revalidated on-demand by path ([`revalidatePath`](#revalidatepath)) or by cache tag ([`revalidateTag`](#fetch-optionsnexttags-and-revalidatetag)).
223	
224	**How On-Demand Revalidation Works**
225	
226	<Image
227	  alt="Diagram showing how on-demand revalidation works, the Data Cache is updated with fresh data after a revalidation request."
228	  srcLight="/docs/light/on-demand-revalidation.png"
229	  srcDark="/docs/dark/on-demand-revalidation.png"
230	  width="1600"
231	  height="1082"
232	/>
233	
234	- The first time a `fetch` request is called, the data will be fetched from the external data source and stored in the Data Cache.
235	- When an on-demand revalidation is triggered, the appropriate cache entries will be purged from the cache.
236	  - This is different from time-based revalidation, which keeps the stale data in the cache until the fresh data is fetched.
237	- The next time a request is made, it will be a cache `MISS` again, and the data will be fetched from the external data source and stored in the Data Cache.
238	
239	### Opting out
240	
241	If you do _not_ want to cache the response from `fetch`, you can do the following:
242	
243	```js
244	let data = await fetch('https://api.vercel.app/blog', { cache: 'no-store' })
245	```
246	
247	## Full Route Cache
248	
249	> **Related terms**:
250	>
251	> You may see the terms **Automatic Static Optimization**, **Static Site Generation**, or **Static Rendering** being used interchangeably to refer to the process of rendering and caching routes of your application at build time.
252	
253	Next.js automatically renders and caches routes at build time. This is an optimization that allows you to serve the cached route instead of rendering on the server for every request, resulting in faster page loads.
254	
255	To understand how the Full Route Cache works, it's helpful to look at how React handles rendering, and how Next.js caches the result:
256	
257	### 1. React Rendering on the Server
258	
259	On the server, Next.js uses React's APIs to orchestrate rendering. The rendering work is split into chunks: by individual routes segments and Suspense boundaries.
260	
261	Each chunk is rendered in two steps:
262	
263	1. React renders Server Components into a special data format, optimized for streaming, called the **React Server Component Payload**.
264	2. Next.js uses the React Server Component Payload and Client Component JavaScript instructions to render **HTML** on the server.
265	
266	This means we don't have to wait for everything to render before caching the work or sending a response. Instead, we can stream a response as work is completed.
267	
268	> **What is the React Server Component Payload?**
269	>
270	> The React Server Component Payload is a compact binary representation of the rendered React Server Components tree. It's used by React on the client to update the browser's DOM. The React Server Component Payload contains:
271	>
272	> - The rendered result of Server Components
273	> - Placeholders for where Client Components should be rendered and references to their JavaScript files
274	> - Any props passed from a Server Component to a Client Component
275	>
276	> To learn more, see the [Server Components](/docs/app/getting-started/server-and-client-components) documentation.
277	
278	### 2. Next.js Caching on the Server (Full Route Cache)
279	
280	<Image
281	  alt="Default behavior of the Full Route Cache, showing how the React Server Component Payload and HTML are cached on the server for statically rendered routes."
282	  srcLight="/docs/light/full-route-cache.png"
283	  srcDark="/docs/dark/full-route-cache.png"
284	  width="1600"
285	  height="888"
286	/>
287	
288	The default behavior of Next.js is to cache the rendered result (React Server Component Payload and HTML) of a route on the server. This applies to statically rendered routes at build time, or during revalidation.
289	
290	### 3. React Hydration and Reconciliation on the Client
291	
292	At request time, on the client:
293	
294	1. The HTML is used to immediately show a fast non-interactive initial preview of the Client and Server Components.
295	2. The React Server Components Payload is used to reconcile the Client and rendered Server Component trees, and update the DOM.
296	3. The JavaScript instructions are used to [hydrate](https://react.dev/reference/react-dom/client/hydrateRoot) Client Components and make the application interactive.
297	
298	### 4. Next.js Caching on the Client (Router Cache)
299	
300	The React Server Component Payload is stored in the client-side [Router Cache](#client-side-router-cache) - a separate in-memory cache, split by individual route segment. This Router Cache is used to improve the navigation experience by storing previously visited routes and prefetching future routes.
301	
302	### 5. Subsequent Navigations
303	
304	On subsequent navigations or during prefetching, Next.js will check if the React Server Components Payload is stored in the Router Cache. If so, it will skip sending a new request to the server.
305	
306	If the route segments are not in the cache, Next.js will fetch the React Server Components Payload from the server, and populate the Router Cache on the client.
307	
308	### Static and Dynamic Rendering
309	
310	Whether a route is cached or not at build time depends on whether it's statically or dynamically rendered. Static routes are cached by default, whereas dynamic routes are rendered at request time, and not cached.
311	
312	This diagram shows the difference between statically and dynamically rendered routes, with cached and uncached data:
313	
314	<Image
315	  alt="How static and dynamic rendering affects the Full Route Cache. Static routes are cached at build time or after data revalidation, whereas dynamic routes are never cached"
316	  srcLight="/docs/light/static-and-dynamic-routes.png"
317	  srcDark="/docs/dark/static-and-dynamic-routes.png"
318	  width="1600"
319	  height="1314"
320	/>
321	
322	Learn more about [static and dynamic rendering](#rendering-strategies).
323	
324	### Duration
325	
326	By default, the Full Route Cache is persistent. This means that the render output is cached across user requests.
327	
328	### Invalidation
329	
330	There are two ways you can invalidate the Full Route Cache:
331	
332	- **[Revalidating Data](/docs/app/guides/caching#revalidating)**: Revalidating the [Data Cache](#data-cache), will in turn invalidate the Router Cache by re-rendering components on the server and caching the new render output.
333	- **Redeploying**: Unlike the Data Cache, which persists across deployments, the Full Route Cache is cleared on new deployments.
334	
335	### Opting out
336	
337	You can opt out of the Full Route Cache, or in other words, dynamically render components for every incoming request, by:
338	
339	- **Using a [Dynamic API](#dynamic-apis)**: This will opt the route out from the Full Route Cache and dynamically render it at request time. The Data Cache can still be used.
340	- **Using the `dynamic = 'force-dynamic'` or `revalidate = 0` route segment config options**: This will skip the Full Route Cache and the Data Cache. Meaning components will be rendered and data fetched on every incoming request to the server. The Router Cache will still apply as it's a client-side cache.
341	- **Opting out of the [Data Cache](#data-cache)**: If a route has a `fetch` request that is not cached, this will opt the route out of the Full Route Cache. The data for the specific `fetch` request will be fetched for every incoming request. Other `fetch` requests that explicitly enable caching will still be cached in the Data Cache. This allows for a hybrid of cached and uncached data.
342	
343	## Client-side Router Cache
344	
345	Next.js has an in-memory client-side router cache that stores the RSC payload of route segments, split by layouts, loading states, and pages.
346	
347	When a user navigates between routes, Next.js caches the visited route segments and [prefetches](/docs/app/getting-started/linking-and-navigating#prefetching) the routes the user is likely to navigate to. This results in instant back/forward navigation, no full-page reload between navigations, and preservation of browser state and React state in shared layouts.
348	
349	With the Router Cache:
350	
351	- **Layouts** are cached and reused on navigation ([partial rendering](/docs/app/getting-started/linking-and-navigating#client-side-transitions)).
352	- **Loading states** are cached and reused on navigation for [instant navigation](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/loading#instant-loading-states).
353	- **Pages** are not cached by default, but are reused during browser backward and forward navigation. You can enable caching for page segments by using the experimental [`staleTimes`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/staleTimes) config option.
354	
355	{/* TODO: Update diagram to match v15 behavior */}
356	
357	> **Good to know:** This cache specifically applies to Next.js and Server Components, and is different to the browser's [bfcache](https://web.dev/bfcache/), though it has a similar result.
358	
359	### Duration
360	
361	The cache is stored in the browser's temporary memory. Two factors determine how long the router cache lasts:
362	
363	- **Session**: The cache persists across navigation. However, it's cleared on page refresh.
364	- **Automatic Invalidation Period**: The cache of layouts and loading states is automatically invalidated after a specific time. The duration depends on how the resource was [prefetched](/docs/app/api-reference/components/link#prefetch), and if the resource was [statically generated](#static-rendering):
365	  - **Default Prefetching** (`prefetch={null}` or unspecified): not cached for dynamic pages, 5 minutes for static pages.
366	  - **Full Prefetching** (`prefetch={true}` or `router.prefetch`): 5 minutes for both static & dynamic pages.
367	
368	While a page refresh will clear **all** cached segments, the automatic invalidation period only affects the individual segment from the time it was prefetched.
369	
370	> **Good to know**: The experimental [`staleTimes`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/staleTimes) config option can be used to adjust the automatic invalidation times mentioned above.
371	
372	### Invalidation
373	
374	There are two ways you can invalidate the Router Cache:
375	
376	- In a **Server Action**:
377	  - Revalidating data on-demand by path with ([`revalidatePath`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidatePath)) or by cache tag with ([`revalidateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag))
378	  - Using [`cookies.set`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cookies#setting-a-cookie) or [`cookies.delete`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cookies#deleting-cookies) invalidates the Router Cache to prevent routes that use cookies from becoming stale (e.g. authentication).
379	- Calling [`router.refresh`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-router) will invalidate the Router Cache and make a new request to the server for the current route.
380	
381	### Opting out
382	
383	As of Next.js 15, page segments are opted out by default.
384	
385	> **Good to know:** You can also opt out of [prefetching](/docs/app/getting-started/linking-and-navigating#prefetching) by setting the `prefetch` prop of the `<Link>` component to `false`.
386	
387	## Cache Interactions
388	
389	When configuring the different caching mechanisms, it's important to understand how they interact with each other:
390	
391	### Data Cache and Full Route Cache
392	
393	- Revalidating or opting out of the Data Cache **will** invalidate the Full Route Cache, as the render output depends on data.
394	- Invalidating or opting out of the Full Route Cache **does not** affect the Data Cache. You can dynamically render a route that has both cached and uncached data. This is useful when most of your page uses cached data, but you have a few components that rely on data that needs to be fetched at request time. You can dynamically render without worrying about the performance impact of re-fetching all the data.
395	
396	### Data Cache and Client-side Router cache
397	
398	- To immediately invalidate the Data Cache and Router cache, you can use [`revalidatePath`](#revalidatepath) or [`revalidateTag`](#fetch-optionsnexttags-and-revalidatetag) in a [Server Action](/docs/app/getting-started/updating-data).
399	- Revalidating the Data Cache in a [Route Handler](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route) **will not** immediately invalidate the Router Cache as the Route Handler isn't tied to a specific route. This means Router Cache will continue to serve the previous payload until a hard refresh, or the automatic invalidation period has elapsed.
400	
401	## APIs
402	
403	The following table provides an overview of how different Next.js APIs affect caching:
404	
405	| API                                                                     | Router Cache               | Full Route Cache      | Data Cache            | React Cache          |
406	| ----------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------- | --------------------- | --------------------- | -------------------- |
407	| [`<Link prefetch>`](#link)                                              | Cache                      |                       |                       |                      |
408	| [`router.prefetch`](#routerprefetch)                                    | Cache                      |                       |                       |                      |
409	| [`router.refresh`](#routerrefresh)                                      | Revalidate                 |                       |                       |                      |
410	| [`fetch`](#fetch)                                                       |                            |                       | Cache                 | Cache (GET and HEAD) |
411	| [`fetch` `options.cache`](#fetch-optionscache)                          |                            |                       | Cache or Opt out      |                      |
412	| [`fetch` `options.next.revalidate`](#fetch-optionsnextrevalidate)       |                            | Revalidate            | Revalidate            |                      |
413	| [`fetch` `options.next.tags`](#fetch-optionsnexttags-and-revalidatetag) |                            | Cache                 | Cache                 |                      |
414	| [`revalidateTag`](#fetch-optionsnexttags-and-revalidatetag)             | Revalidate (Server Action) | Revalidate            | Revalidate            |                      |
415	| [`revalidatePath`](#revalidatepath)                                     | Revalidate (Server Action) | Revalidate            | Revalidate            |                      |
416	| [`const revalidate`](#segment-config-options)                           |                            | Revalidate or Opt out | Revalidate or Opt out |                      |
417	| [`const dynamic`](#segment-config-options)                              |                            | Cache or Opt out      | Cache or Opt out      |                      |
418	| [`cookies`](#cookies)                                                   | Revalidate (Server Action) | Opt out               |                       |                      |
419	| [`headers`, `searchParams`](#dynamic-apis)                              |                            | Opt out               |                       |                      |
420	| [`generateStaticParams`](#generatestaticparams)                         |                            | Cache                 |                       |                      |
421	| [`React.cache`](#react-cache-function)                                  |                            |                       |                       | Cache                |
422	| [`unstable_cache`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/unstable_cache)    |                            |                       | Cache                 |                      |
423	
424	### `<Link>`
425	
426	By default, the `<Link>` component automatically prefetches routes from the Full Route Cache and adds the React Server Component Payload to the Router Cache.
427	
428	To disable prefetching, you can set the `prefetch` prop to `false`. But this will not skip the cache permanently, the route segment will still be cached client-side when the user visits the route.
429	
430	Learn more about the [`<Link>` component](/docs/app/api-reference/components/link).
431	
432	### `router.prefetch`
433	
434	The `prefetch` option of the `useRouter` hook can be used to manually prefetch a route. This adds the React Server Component Payload to the Router Cache.
435	
436	See the [`useRouter` hook](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-router) API reference.
437	
438	### `router.refresh`
439	
440	The `refresh` option of the `useRouter` hook can be used to manually refresh a route. This completely clears the Router Cache, and makes a new request to the server for the current route. `refresh` does not affect the Data or Full Route Cache.
441	
442	The rendered result will be reconciled on the client while preserving React state and browser state.
443	
444	See the [`useRouter` hook](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-router) API reference.
445	
446	### `fetch`
447	
448	Data returned from `fetch` is _not_ automatically cached in the Data Cache.
449	
450	By default, when no `cache` or `next.revalidate` options are provided:
451	
452	- [Dynamic rendering](#dynamic-rendering): Fetch runs on every request and always returns fresh data.
453	- [Static rendering](#static-rendering): Fetched data is stored in the [Data Cache](#data-cache), and the rendered output in the [Full Route Cache](#full-route-cache). Next.js serves this cached result until the path is revalidated.
454	
455	See the [`fetch` API Reference](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/fetch) for more options.
456	
457	### `fetch options.cache`
458	
459	You can opt individual `fetch` into caching by setting the `cache` option to `force-cache`:
460	
461	```jsx
462	// Opt into caching
463	fetch(`https://...`, { cache: 'force-cache' })
464	```
465	
466	See the [`fetch` API Reference](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/fetch) for more options.
467	
468	### `fetch options.next.revalidate`
469	
470	You can use the `next.revalidate` option of `fetch` to set the revalidation period (in seconds) of an individual `fetch` request. This will revalidate the Data Cache, which in turn will revalidate the Full Route Cache. Fresh data will be fetched, and components will be re-rendered on the server.
471	
472	```jsx
473	// Revalidate at most after 1 hour
474	fetch(`https://...`, { next: { revalidate: 3600 } })
475	```
476	
477	See the [`fetch` API reference](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/fetch) for more options.
478	
479	### `fetch options.next.tags` and `revalidateTag`
480	
481	Next.js has a cache tagging system for fine-grained data caching and revalidation.
482	
483	1. When using `fetch` or [`unstable_cache`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/unstable_cache), you have the option to tag cache entries with one or more tags.
484	2. Then, you can call `revalidateTag` to purge the cache entries associated with that tag.
485	
486	For example, you can set a tag when fetching data:
487	
488	```jsx
489	// Cache data with a tag
490	fetch(`https://...`, { next: { tags: ['a', 'b', 'c'] } })
491	```
492	
493	Then, call `revalidateTag` with a tag to purge the cache entry:
494	
495	```jsx
496	// Revalidate entries with a specific tag
497	revalidateTag('a')
498	```
499	
500	There are two places you can use `revalidateTag`, depending on what you're trying to achieve:
501	
502	1. [Route Handlers](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route) - to revalidate data in response of a third party event (e.g. webhook). This will not invalidate the Router Cache immediately as the Router Handler isn't tied to a specific route.
503	2. [Server Actions](/docs/app/getting-started/updating-data) - to revalidate data after a user action (e.g. form submission). This will invalidate the Router Cache for the associated route.
504	
505	### `revalidatePath`
506	
507	`revalidatePath` allows you manually revalidate data **and** re-render the route segments below a specific path in a single operation. Calling the `revalidatePath` method revalidates the Data Cache, which in turn invalidates the Full Route Cache.
508	
509	```jsx
510	revalidatePath('/')
511	```
512	
513	There are two places you can use `revalidatePath`, depending on what you're trying to achieve:
514	
515	1. [Route Handlers](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route) - to revalidate data in response to a third party event (e.g. webhook).
516	2. [Server Actions](/docs/app/getting-started/updating-data) - to revalidate data after a user interaction (e.g. form submission, clicking a button).
517	
518	See the [`revalidatePath` API reference](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidatePath) for more information.
519	
520	> **`revalidatePath`** vs. **`router.refresh`**:
521	>
522	> Calling `router.refresh` will clear the Router cache, and re-render route segments on the server without invalidating the Data Cache or the Full Route Cache.
523	>
524	> The difference is that `revalidatePath` purges the Data Cache and Full Route Cache, whereas `router.refresh()` does not change the Data Cache and Full Route Cache, as it is a client-side API.
525	
526	### Dynamic APIs
527	
528	Dynamic APIs like `cookies` and `headers`, and the `searchParams` prop in Pages depend on runtime incoming request information. Using them will opt a route out of the Full Route Cache, in other words, the route will be dynamically rendered.
529	
530	#### `cookies`
531	
532	Using `cookies.set` or `cookies.delete` in a Server Action invalidates the Router Cache to prevent routes that use cookies from becoming stale (e.g. to reflect authentication changes).
533	
534	See the [`cookies`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cookies) API reference.
535	
536	### Segment Config Options
537	
538	The Route Segment Config options can be used to override the route segment defaults or when you're not able to use the `fetch` API (e.g. database client or 3rd party libraries).
539	
540	The following Route Segment Config options will opt out of the Full Route Cache:
541	
542	- `const dynamic = 'force-dynamic'`
543	
544	This config option will opt all fetches out of the Data Cache (i.e. `no-store`):
545	
546	- `const fetchCache = 'default-no-store'`
547	
548	See the [`fetchCache`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route-segment-config#fetchcache) to see more advanced options.
549	
550	See the [Route Segment Config](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route-segment-config) documentation for more options.
551	
552	### `generateStaticParams`
553	
554	For [dynamic segments](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) (e.g. `app/blog/[slug]/page.js`), paths provided by `generateStaticParams` are cached in the Full Route Cache at build time. At request time, Next.js will also cache paths that weren't known at build time the first time they're visited.
555	
556	To statically render all paths at build time, supply the full list of paths to `generateStaticParams`:
557	
558	```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js"
559	export async function generateStaticParams() {
560	  const posts = await fetch('https://.../posts').then((res) => res.json())
561	
562	  return posts.map((post) => ({
563	    slug: post.slug,
564	  }))
565	}
566	```
567	
568	To statically render a subset of paths at build time, and the rest the first time they're visited at runtime, return a partial list of paths:
569	
570	```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js"
571	export async function generateStaticParams() {
572	  const posts = await fetch('https://.../posts').then((res) => res.json())
573	
574	  // Render the first 10 posts at build time
575	  return posts.slice(0, 10).map((post) => ({
576	    slug: post.slug,
577	  }))
578	}
579	```
580	
581	To statically render all paths the first time they're visited, return an empty array (no paths will be rendered at build time) or utilize [`export const dynamic = 'force-static'`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route-segment-config#dynamic):
582	
583	```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js"
584	export async function generateStaticParams() {
585	  return []
586	}
587	```
588	
589	> **Good to know:** You must return an array from `generateStaticParams`, even if it's empty. Otherwise, the route will be dynamically rendered.
590	
591	```jsx filename="app/changelog/[slug]/page.js"
592	export const dynamic = 'force-static'
593	```
594	
595	To disable caching at request time, add the `export const dynamicParams = false` option in a route segment. When this config option is used, only paths provided by `generateStaticParams` will be served, and other routes will 404 or match (in the case of [catch-all routes](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes#catch-all-segments)).
596	
597	### React `cache` function
598	
599	The React `cache` function allows you to memoize the return value of a function, allowing you to call the same function multiple times while only executing it once.
600	
601	`fetch` requests using the `GET` or `HEAD` methods are automatically memoized, so you do not need to wrap it in React `cache`. However, for other `fetch` methods, or when using data fetching libraries (such as some database, CMS, or GraphQL clients) that don't inherently memoize requests, you can use `cache` to manually memoize data requests.
602	
603	```ts filename="utils/get-item.ts" switcher
604	import { cache } from 'react'
605	import db from '@/lib/db'
606	
607	export const getItem = cache(async (id: string) => {
608	  const item = await db.item.findUnique({ id })
609	  return item
610	})
611	```
612	
613	```js filename="utils/get-item.js" switcher
614	import { cache } from 'react'
615	import db from '@/lib/db'
616	
617	export const getItem = cache(async (id) => {
618	  const item = await db.item.findUnique({ id })
619	  return item
620	})
621	```

2	title: updateTag
3	description: API Reference for the updateTag function.
4	---
5	
6	`updateTag` allows you to update [cached data](/docs/app/guides/caching) on-demand for a specific cache tag from within [Server Actions](/docs/app/getting-started/updating-data).
7	
8	This function is designed for **read-your-own-writes** scenarios, where a user makes a change (like creating a post), and the UI immediately shows the change, rather than stale data.
9	
10	## Usage
11	
12	`updateTag` can **only** be called from within [Server Actions](/docs/app/getting-started/updating-data). It cannot be used in Route Handlers, Client Components, or any other context.
13	
14	If you need to invalidate cache tags in Route Handlers or other contexts, use [`revalidateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag) instead.
15	
16	> **Good to know**: `updateTag` immediately expires the cached data for the specified tag. The next request will wait to fetch fresh data rather than serving stale content from the cache, ensuring users see their changes immediately.
17	
18	## Parameters
19	
20	```tsx
21	updateTag(tag: string): void;
22	```
23	
24	- `tag`: A string representing the cache tag associated with the data you want to update. Must not exceed 256 characters. This value is case-sensitive.
25	
26	Tags must first be assigned to cached data. You can do this in two ways:
27	
28	- Using the [`next.tags`](/docs/app/guides/caching#fetch-optionsnexttags-and-revalidatetag) option with `fetch` for caching external API requests:
29	
30	```tsx
31	fetch(url, { next: { tags: ['posts'] } })
32	```
33	
34	- Using [`cacheTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag) inside cached functions or components with the `'use cache'` directive:
35	
36	```tsx
37	import { cacheTag } from 'next/cache'
38	
39	async function getData() {
40	  'use cache'
41	  cacheTag('posts')
42	  // ...
43	}
44	```
45	
46	## Returns
47	
48	`updateTag` does not return a value.
49	
50	## Differences from revalidateTag
51	
52	While both `updateTag` and `revalidateTag` invalidate cached data, they serve different purposes:
53	
54	- **`updateTag`**:
55	  - Can only be used in Server Actions
56	  - Next request waits for fresh data (no stale content served)
57	  - Designed for read-your-own-writes scenarios
58	
59	- **`revalidateTag`**:
60	  - Can be used in Server Actions and Route Handlers
61	  - With `profile="max"` (recommended): Serves cached data while fetching fresh data in the background (stale-while-revalidate)
62	  - With custom profile: Can be configured to any cache life profile for advanced usage
63	  - Without profile: legacy behavior which is equivalent to `updateTag`
64	
65	## Examples
66	
67	### Server Action with Read-Your-Own-Writes
68	
69	```ts filename="app/actions.ts" switcher
70	'use server'
71	
72	import { updateTag } from 'next/cache'
73	import { redirect } from 'next/navigation'
74	
75	export async function createPost(formData: FormData) {
76	  const title = formData.get('title')
77	  const content = formData.get('content')
78	
79	  // Create the post in your database
80	  const post = await db.post.create({
81	    data: { title, content },
82	  })
83	
84	  // Invalidate cache tags so the new post is immediately visible
85	  // 'posts' tag: affects any page that displays a list of posts
86	  updateTag('posts')
87	  // 'post-{id}' tag: affects the individual post detail page
88	  updateTag(`post-${post.id}`)
89	
90	  // Redirect to the new post - user will see fresh data, not cached
91	  redirect(`/posts/${post.id}`)
92	}
93	```
94	
95	```js filename="app/actions.js" switcher
96	'use server'
97	
98	import { updateTag } from 'next/cache'
99	import { redirect } from 'next/navigation'
100	
101	export async function createPost(formData) {
102	  const title = formData.get('title')
103	  const content = formData.get('content')
104	
105	  // Create the post in your database
106	  const post = await db.post.create({
107	    data: { title, content },
108	  })
109	
110	  // Invalidate cache tags so the new post is immediately visible
111	  // 'posts' tag: affects any page that displays a list of posts
112	  updateTag('posts')
113	  // 'post-{id}' tag: affects the individual post detail page
114	  updateTag(`post-${post.id}`)
115	
116	  // Redirect to the new post - user will see fresh data, not cached
117	  redirect(`/posts/${post.id}`)
118	}
119	```
120	
121	### Error when used outside Server Actions
122	
123	```ts filename="app/api/posts/route.ts" switcher
124	import { updateTag } from 'next/cache'
125	
126	export async function POST() {
127	  // This will throw an error
128	  updateTag('posts')
129	  // Error: updateTag can only be called from within a Server Action
130	
131	  // Use revalidateTag instead in Route Handlers
132	  revalidateTag('posts', 'max')
133	}
134	```
135	
136	## When to use updateTag
137	
138	Use `updateTag` when:
139	
140	- You're in a Server Action
141	- You need immediate cache invalidation for read-your-own-writes
142	- You want to ensure the next request sees updated data
143	
144	Use `revalidateTag` instead when:
145	
146	- You're in a Route Handler or other non-action context
147	- You want stale-while-revalidate semantics
148	- You're building a webhook or API endpoint for cache invalidation
149	
150	## Related
151	
152	- [`revalidateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag) - For invalidating tags in Route Handlers
153	- [`revalidatePath`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidatePath) - For invalidating specific paths

1	---
2	title: revalidateTag
3	description: API Reference for the revalidateTag function.
4	---
5	
6	`revalidateTag` allows you to invalidate [cached data](/docs/app/guides/caching) on-demand for a specific cache tag.
7	
8	This function is ideal for content where a slight delay in updates is acceptable, such as blog posts, product catalogs, or documentation. Users receive stale content while fresh data loads in the background.
9	
10	## Usage
11	
12	`revalidateTag` can be called in Server Functions and Route Handlers.
13	
14	`revalidateTag` cannot be called in Client Components or Proxy, as it only works in server environments.
15	
16	### Revalidation Behavior
17	
18	The revalidation behavior depends on whether you provide the second argument:
19	
20	- **With `profile="max"` (recommended)**: The tag entry is marked as stale, and the next time a resource with that tag is visited, it will use stale-while-revalidate semantics. This means the stale content is served while fresh content is fetched in the background.
21	- **With a custom cache life profile**: For advanced usage, you can specify any cache life profile that your application has defined, allowing for custom revalidation behaviors tailored to your specific caching requirements.
22	- **Without the second argument (deprecated)**: The tag entry is expired immediately, and the next request to that resource will be a blocking revalidate/cache miss. This behavior is now deprecated, and you should either use `profile="max"` or migrate to [`updateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/updateTag).
23	
24	> **Good to know**: When using `profile="max"`, `revalidateTag` marks tagged data as stale, but fresh data is only fetched when pages using that tag are next visited. This means calling `revalidateTag` will not immediately trigger many revalidations at once. The invalidation only happens when any page using that tag is next visited.
25	
26	## Parameters
27	
28	```ts
29	revalidateTag(tag: string, profile: string | { expire?: number }): void;
30	```
31	
32	- `tag`: A string representing the cache tag associated with the data you want to revalidate. Must not exceed 256 characters. This value is case-sensitive.
33	- `profile`: A string that specifies the revalidation behavior. The recommended value is `"max"` which provides stale-while-revalidate semantics, or any of the other default or custom profiles defined in [`cacheLife`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheLife). Alternatively, you can pass an object with an `expire` property for custom expiration behavior.
34	
35	Tags must first be assigned to cached data. You can do this in two ways:
36	
37	- Using the [`next.tags`](/docs/app/guides/caching#fetch-optionsnexttags-and-revalidatetag) option with `fetch` for caching external API requests:
38	
39	```tsx
40	fetch(url, { next: { tags: ['posts'] } })
41	```
42	
43	- Using [`cacheTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag) inside cached functions or components with the `'use cache'` directive:
44	
45	```tsx
46	import { cacheTag } from 'next/cache'
47	
48	async function getData() {
49	  'use cache'
50	  cacheTag('posts')
51	  // ...
52	}
53	```
54	
55	> **Good to know**: The single-argument form `revalidateTag(tag)` is deprecated. It currently works if TypeScript errors are suppressed, but this behavior may be removed in a future version. Update to the two-argument signature.
56	
57	## Returns
58	
59	`revalidateTag` does not return a value.
60	
61	## Relationship with `revalidatePath`
62	
63	`revalidateTag` invalidates data with specific tags across all pages that use those tags, while [`revalidatePath`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidatePath) invalidates specific page or layout paths.
64	
65	> **Good to know**: These functions serve different purposes and may need to be used together for comprehensive data consistency. For detailed examples and considerations, see [relationship with revalidateTag and updateTag](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidatePath#relationship-with-revalidatetag-and-updatetag) for more information.
66	
67	## Examples
68	
69	The following examples demonstrate how to use `revalidateTag` in different contexts. In both cases, we're using `profile="max"` to mark data as stale and use stale-while-revalidate semantics, which is the recommended approach for most use cases.
70	
71	### Server Action
72	
73	```ts filename="app/actions.ts" switcher
74	'use server'
75	
76	import { revalidateTag } from 'next/cache'
77	
78	export default async function submit() {
79	  await addPost()
80	  revalidateTag('posts', 'max')
81	}
82	```
83	
84	```js filename="app/actions.js" switcher
85	'use server'
86	
87	import { revalidateTag } from 'next/cache'
88	
89	export default async function submit() {
90	  await addPost()
91	  revalidateTag('posts', 'max')
92	}
93	```
94	
95	### Route Handler
96	
97	```ts filename="app/api/revalidate/route.ts" switcher
98	import type { NextRequest } from 'next/server'
99	import { revalidateTag } from 'next/cache'
100	
101	export async function GET(request: NextRequest) {
102	  const tag = request.nextUrl.searchParams.get('tag')
103	
104	  if (tag) {
105	    revalidateTag(tag, 'max')
106	    return Response.json({ revalidated: true, now: Date.now() })
107	  }
108	
109	  return Response.json({
110	    revalidated: false,
111	    now: Date.now(),
112	    message: 'Missing tag to revalidate',
113	  })
114	}
115	```
116	
117	```js filename="app/api/revalidate/route.js" switcher
118	import { revalidateTag } from 'next/cache'
119	
120	export async function GET(request) {
121	  const tag = request.nextUrl.searchParams.get('tag')
122	
123	  if (tag) {
124	    revalidateTag(tag, 'max')
125	    return Response.json({ revalidated: true, now: Date.now() })
126	  }
127	
128	  return Response.json({
129	    revalidated: false,
130	    now: Date.now(),
131	    message: 'Missing tag to revalidate',
132	  })
133	}
134	```
135	
136	> **Good to know**: For webhooks or third-party services that need immediate expiration, you can pass `{ expire: 0 }` as the second argument: `revalidateTag(tag, { expire: 0 })`. This pattern is necessary when external systems call your Route Handlers and require data to expire immediately. For all other cases, it's recommended to use [`updateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/updateTag) in Server Actions for immediate updates instead.

2	title: cacheTag
3	description: Learn how to use the cacheTag function to manage cache invalidation in your Next.js application.
4	related:

13	
14	The `cacheTag` function allows you to tag cached data for on-demand invalidation. By associating tags with cache entries, you can selectively purge or revalidate specific cache entries without affecting other cached data.
15	
16	## Usage
17	
18	To use `cacheTag`, enable the [`cacheComponents` flag](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheComponents) in your `next.config.js` file:
19	
20	```ts filename="next.config.ts" switcher
21	import type { NextConfig } from 'next'
22	
23	const nextConfig: NextConfig = {
24	  cacheComponents: true,
25	}
26	
27	export default nextConfig
28	```
29	
30	```js filename="next.config.js" switcher
31	const nextConfig = {
32	  cacheComponents: true,
33	}
34	
35	export default nextConfig
36	```
37	
38	The `cacheTag` function takes one or more string values.
39	
40	```tsx filename="app/data.ts" switcher
41	import { cacheTag } from 'next/cache'
42	
43	export async function getData() {
44	  'use cache'
45	  cacheTag('my-data')
46	  const data = await fetch('/api/data')
47	  return data
48	}
49	```
50	
51	```jsx filename="app/data.js" switcher
52	import { cacheTag } from 'next/cache'
53	
54	export async function getData() {
55	  'use cache'
56	  cacheTag('my-data')
57	  const data = await fetch('/api/data')
58	  return data
59	}
60	```
61	
62	You can then purge the cache on-demand using [`revalidateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag) API in another function, for example, a [route handler](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route) or [Server Action](/docs/app/getting-started/updating-data):
63	
64	```tsx filename="app/action.ts" switcher
65	'use server'
66	
67	import { revalidateTag } from 'next/cache'
68	
69	export default async function submit() {
70	  await addPost()
71	  revalidateTag('my-data')
72	}
73	```
74	
75	```jsx filename="app/action.js" switcher
76	'use server'
77	
78	import { revalidateTag } from 'next/cache'
79	
80	export default async function submit() {
81	  await addPost()
82	  revalidateTag('my-data')
83	}
84	```
85	
86	## Good to know
87	
88	- **Idempotent Tags**: Applying the same tag multiple times has no additional effect.
89	- **Multiple Tags**: You can assign multiple tags to a single cache entry by passing multiple string values to `cacheTag`.
90	
91	```tsx
92	cacheTag('tag-one', 'tag-two')
93	```
94	
95	- **Limits**: The max length for a custom tag is 256 characters and the max tag items is 128.
96	
97	## Examples
98	
99	### Tagging components or functions
100	
101	Tag your cached data by calling `cacheTag` within a cached function or component:
102	
103	```tsx filename="app/components/bookings.tsx" switcher
104	import { cacheTag } from 'next/cache'
105	
106	interface BookingsProps {
107	  type: string
108	}
109	
110	export async function Bookings({ type = 'haircut' }: BookingsProps) {
111	  'use cache'
112	  cacheTag('bookings-data')
113	
114	  async function getBookingsData() {
115	    const data = await fetch(`/api/bookings?type=${encodeURIComponent(type)}`)
116	    return data
117	  }
118	
119	  return //...
120	}
121	```
122	
123	```jsx filename="app/components/bookings.js" switcher
124	import { cacheTag } from 'next/cache'
125	
126	export async function Bookings({ type = 'haircut' }) {
127	  'use cache'
128	  cacheTag('bookings-data')
129	
130	  async function getBookingsData() {
131	    const data = await fetch(`/api/bookings?type=${encodeURIComponent(type)}`)
132	    return data
133	  }
134	
135	  return //...
136	}
137	```
138	
139	### Creating tags from external data
140	
141	You can use the data returned from an async function to tag the cache entry.
142	
143	```tsx filename="app/components/bookings.tsx" switcher
144	import { cacheTag } from 'next/cache'
145	
146	interface BookingsProps {
147	  type: string
148	}

152	    'use cache'
153	    const data = await fetch(`/api/bookings?type=${encodeURIComponent(type)}`)
154	    cacheTag('bookings-data', data.id)
155	    return data
156	  }
157	  return //...
158	}
159	```

## Solution

### Files created/modified

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `lib/posts.ts` | Data store with `getPosts()` (cached) and `createPost()` |
| `app/actions.ts` | **Server Action** with `updateTag('posts')` |
| `next.config.ts` | Enabled `cacheComponents: true` for `'use cache'` support |
| `app/page.tsx` | UI — form to create posts + cached posts list |

### Key design decisions

**`updateTag('posts')`** is the correct cache invalidation function here, not `revalidateTag`. Here's why:

- **`updateTag`** — Server Actions only. Immediately expires the cached data. The *next request waits for fresh data* — no stale content is served. This is the "read-your-own-writes" pattern.
- **`revalidateTag(tag, 'max')`** — Uses stale-while-revalidate: serves old cached content while fetching fresh data in the background. That would mean the user sees stale data on the next page load, which you explicitly said is unacceptable.

From the Next.js 16 docs:

> `updateTag` immediately expires the cached data for the specified tag. The next request will wait to fetch fresh data rather than serving stale content from the cache, ensuring users see their changes immediately.

### How it works

1. **Caching layer** — `lib/posts.ts` exports `getPosts()` with `'use cache'` and `cacheTag('posts')`. The Data Cache stores the result under the `"posts"` tag.
2. **Server Action** (`app/actions.ts`) — `createPostAction` writes the new post to the store, then calls `updateTag('posts')` to immediately expire the cached data.
3. **Page** (`app/page.tsx`) — `Home` calls `getPosts()`, which hits the cache. After a post is created, `updateTag` expires that cache, so the next load of the page fetches fresh data.

### To run

```bash
npm run dev
```

The form at `http://localhost:3000` will create posts, and every submission guarantees the next page load shows fresh data — never stale cached content.
